content.assessment: A passive baseline is analytically useful as a counterfactual but from an equity lens it institutionalizes present harms: continued opaque SEN growth (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02) under contested causal explanations (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03), persistent regional specialist shortages that fall hardest on already-underserved counties (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05), and continued interaction of free school choice with Roma segregation dynamics (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08). Doing nothing does not freeze the status quo; it allows sorting mechanisms already in motion to continue reallocating access and quality along existing lines, particularly given the documented shift of SNI status from an inclusion lever to an exclusionary admissions factor (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10).
opportunities: Could be paired with independent monitoring to at least document worsening distributional trends for future action
strengths: Provides a transparent counterfactual against which distributional harms of inaction can be measured
Avoids introducing new implementation-driven equity risks since no structural change occurs
threats: Continued interaction of free school choice and Roma overrepresentation in referral/segregation channels may deepen without any corrective mechanism
Demographic and workforce drift could increase de facto segregation by resource capacity rather than need
weaknesses: Allows continued unexplained SEN growth, especially in the fastest-growing BTMN-adjacent subcategory, without addressing underlying drivers
Leaves regionally concentrated workforce shortages unaddressed, compounding access gaps for underserved counties
Does not interrupt the documented drift of SNI status from inclusion-enabling to exclusion-enabling in admissions
